Eur J Biochem 58, 523-531 (1975) 
Mitochondria and Peroxisomes 
from the Cellular Slime Mould Dictyostelium discoideum 
Isolation Techniques and Urate Oxidase Association with Peroxisomes 
Roger W PARISH 
Cytologisches Laboratorium, Institut fur allgemeine Botanik, Universitat Zurich 
(Received March 24/ June 30, 1975) 
The isolation of cell organelles from Dictyostelium discoideum was attempted using a variety of 
techaiques Cell homogenization (e.g Potter-Elvehjem, glass beads) gave poor yields of organelles 
which were, in addition, exceptionally fragile and unstable in density gradients 
An isolation method was developed using Triton X-100 in buffered sorbitol/Ficoll solutions at 
concentrations optimal for plasma membrane rupture Immediately following cell lysis the solutions 
were diluted to sub-optimal Triton X-100 concentrations Sedimentabilities of malate dehydrogenase, 
citrate synthetase, urate oxidase and catalase of around 55 x, 40 %, 35 % and 55 % respectively could 
be demonstrated using this method The organelles were more resistant to breakage during resuspen- 
sion following differential centrifugation and remained largely intact during density gradient centri- 
fugation The distribution of adenylate kinase activity in gradients showed that at least half the mito- 
chondria retained an intact outer membrane 
The mitochondria and peroxisomes could not be clearly separated using conventional sucrose- 
Ficoll density gradients 
Separation was achieved by incubating the cell homogenate with succinate and a tetrazolium dye 
(2-p-iodophenyl-3-p-nitrophenyl-5-phenyl monotetrazolium chloride) Succinate dehydrogenase 
activity of mitochondria reduced the tetrazolium dye and the product (formazan) was deposited on 
the mitochondria1 membranes (“heavy-labelling”) The mitochondria then sedimented to denser 
regions of the gradient while catalase distribution remained unchanged The treatment left both 
organelles intact 
The mitochondria (1.21 g/ml) were slightly denser than the peroxisomes (1.19 g/ml) The peroxi- 
somes contained catalase and urate oxidase ; no other hydrogen-peroxide-producing oxidases were 
detected 
The slime mould urate oxidase resembled the mammalian enzyme It had an apparent K, value 
of 12.5 yM, an optimum of activity at pH 8.5 in borate buffer and was competitively inhibited by 
trichloropurine 
The cellular slime moulds are simple eukaryotes 
and undergo differentiation In the absence of nutrients 
the vegetative amoebae aggregate and differentiate 
into two major cell types, stalk and spore cells [l] 
Defhirion Asoo unit, the quantity of material Contained in 
1 ml of a solution which has an absorbance of 1 at 600 nm, when 
measured in a l-cm path-length cell 
Enzymes 8-N-Acetylglucosaminidase (EC 3.2.1.30); catalase 
(EC 1.11.1.6); malate dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.37); citrate syn- 
thase (EC 4.1.3.7); urate: 0, oxidoreductase (EC 1.7.3.3); acid ~- - This differentiation can be synchronised in the labo- 
ratory [2] 
The cellular slime moulds may prove useful 
organisms for studying the biogenesis of cell organelles 
and their role in cell differentiation [3] In the following 
paper, methods for isolating cell organelles are de- 
scribed and compared Marker enzymes were used to 
estimate intactness of the organelles Further, a 
number of enzymes often found associated with animal 
and plant peroxisomes were sought in cell fractions - 
phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.2); alkaline phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.1); ade- and gradients 
The methods were developed when we found that nylate kinase (EC 2.7.4.3): glycollic acid oxidase (EC 1.1.3.1): 
isocitratelyase(EC 4.1.3.1): malate synthase (EC 4.1.3.2); u-amino- 
acid oxidase (EC 1.4.3.3): NADH-dichlorophenolindophenoloxido- conventional homogenization techniques and density 
reductase (EC 1.6.99.3) gradients were inadequate for the isolation and 524 Mitochondria and Peroxisomes from Dictyostelium 
separation of intact organelles Similar methods have 
been successfully used for the isolation of intact 
nuclei [4] 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
Cell Cultures 
Strain Ax-3 amoebae (from W F Loomis, Uni- 
versity of California, San Diego) were grown axenically 
in HL 5 medium (10 g Difco proteose-peptone, 10 g 
glucose, 5 g Difco yeast extract, 0.43 g Na,HPO, 
 2 H20 and 0.35 g KH2P04 per litre of distilled water) 
Amoebae were washed twice with PDF solution 
(0.02 M KCI, 0.0025 M MgSO,, 0.5 g/1 streptomycin 
sulfate, pH 6.5) [5, 61 sedimenting in a Christ Simplex 
bench centrifuge for 60 s at 350 x g 
Cell Rupture 
The cells were ruptured in a number of ways and 
the medium preferred was sorbitol/Ficoll medium 
(0.5 M sorbitol, 2.5% Ficoll (w/v) in 0.05 M Tris- 
HCl buffer, pH 7.4) [7,8] 
Potter Elvehjem Homogenizer The cells (1 : 30, v/v, 
in sorbitol/Ficoll medium) were homogenized with 
10 strokes of a Potter Elvehjem glass homogenizer 
with a Teflon pestle (15 ml; R Brauns, Melsungen) 
attached to a Bosch electric drill (3100 rev./min) 
The unbroken cells were then removed by sedimenting 
(800 x g, 60 s), resuspended and the homogenization 
repeated The supernatants were combined and used 
for differential centrifugation 
Liquid Nitrogen The cells were frozen by adding 
liquid nitrogen Addition of sorbitol/Ficoll medium 
and rethawing did not lead to significant cell breakage 
unless the thawing cells were also ground with a pestle 
and mortar Whole cells were removed and the super- 
natant used for differential centrifugation

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article: Eur J Biochem 58, 523-531 (1975)**
**Title: Mitochondria and Peroxisomes from the Cellular Slime Mould Dictyostelium discoideum Isolation Techniques and Urate Oxidase Association with Peroxisomes**
**Author: Roger W PARISH**

**Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:**
1. "An isolation method was developed using Triton X-100 in buffered sorbitol/Ficoll solutions at concentrations optimal for plasma membrane rupture."
2. "Immediately following cell lysis the solutions were diluted to sub-optimal Triton X-100 concentrations."
3. "Sedimentabilities of malate dehydrogenase, citrate synthetase, urate oxidase, and catalase of around 55 x, 40 %, 35 %, and 55 % respectively could be demonstrated using this method."
4. "The organelles were more resistant to breakage during resuspension following differential centrifugation and remained largely intact during density gradient centrifugation."
5. "The distribution of adenylate kinase activity in gradients showed that at least half the mitochondria retained an intact outer membrane."
6. "Separation was achieved by incubating the cell homogenate with succinate and a tetrazolium dye (2-p-iodophenyl-3-p-nitrophenyl-5-phenyl monotetrazolium chloride)."
7. "Succinate dehydrogenase activity of mitochondria reduced the tetrazolium dye and the product (formazan) was deposited on the mitochondrial membranes ('heavy-labelling')."
8. "The mitochondria then sedimented to denser regions of the gradient while catalase distribution remained unchanged."
9. "The mitochondria (1.21 g/ml) were slightly denser than the peroxisomes (1.19 g/ml)."
10. "The peroxisomes contained catalase and urate oxidase; no other hydrogen-peroxide-producing oxidases were detected."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide specific details about the Triton X-100 concentrations used.
- The article does not mention the specific centrifugation speeds or times used for differential centrifugation and density gradient centrifugation.
- The article does not provide information on the specific method used for cell lysis or the specific sucrose-Ficoll density gradients used for separation.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed method for isolating mitochondria from Dictyostelium discoideum using Triton X-100 in buffered sorbitol/Ficoll solutions. The method demonstrates the sedimentabilities of various enzymes and the resistance of organelles to breakage during centrifugation. However, the article lacks specific details on Triton X-100 concentrations, centrifugation speeds and times, cell lysis method, and sucrose-Ficoll density gradients. These gaps in the protocol limit the ability to replicate the isolation method accurately.

summary:
Revised Summary:
A study was conducted on the isolation and association of organelles in slime mold cells from Dictyostelium discoideum. Various techniques were used to isolate cell organelles, but they yielded poor results. A new method using Triton X-100 in buffered sorbitol/Ficoll solutions was developed, resulting in successful isolation of intact organelles. The density gradient revealed that mitochondria were slightly denser than peroxisomes. Enzymes such as malate dehydrogenase, citrate synthetase, urate oxidase, and catalase were present in slime mold cells. The slime mold urate oxidase was similar to the mammalian enzyme and inhibited by trichloropurine. The study also highlighted the differentiation of slime mold cells into stalk and spore cells. Cellular slime molds are simple eukaryotes that can be used to study organelle biogenesis and cell differentiation.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The cells were homogenized in a Vibrogen cell mill with glass beads at 0 °C for 3 x 15 s.
2. "0.3 g fresh weight of cells were suspended in 1.0 ml of distilled water in a 12-ml glass centrifuge tube."
3. "After 5 min, 3 ml of sorbitol/Ficoll medium containing various Triton X-100 concentrations was added."
4. "The solution was decanted twice and after various times diluted with 6.0 ml of cold sorbitol/Ficoll medium."
5. Differential centrifugations were carried out according to the scheme shown in Figure 1.
6. Gradients consisting of sorbitol/Ficoll medium or sucrose and 2% Ficoll in 0.05 M Tris-HCl buffer were prepared.
7. The gradients were centrifuged in an ultracentrifuge using a SW 51 rotor for 2.5 h at 40000 rev./min.
8. 1 ml of mitochondrial fraction in sorbitol/Ficoll medium was incubated with the tetrazolium dye and sodium succinate.
9. The mixture was layered onto a linear 20% to 60% sucrose gradient containing 2% Ficoll in 0.05 M Tris-HCl buffer.
10. The gradient was centrifuged for 2.5 h at 40000 rev./min.
11. Enzyme assays were performed for catalase, NADH-dichlorophenolindophenol oxidoreductase, malate dehydrogenase, urate oxidase, malate synthase, isocitrate lyase, citrate synthase, glycollate oxidase, protein, D-amino acid oxidase, adenylate kinase, acid phosphatase, and alkaline phosphatase.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed description of the mitochondrial isolation steps. However, there are some protocol gaps and missing details. The exact concentrations of Triton X-100 and the specific dilution times are not mentioned. Additionally, the specific details of the enzyme assays are not provided.

summary: Revised Summary: Cells were homogenized using a Vibrogen cell mill with glass beads at 0°C. Triton X-100 (5 min) and various concentrations were used to rupture cells and obtain the mitochondrial fraction. The solution was diluted with cold sorbitol/Ficoll medium. Differential and gradient centrifugation were performed to isolate different cell fractions. Mitochondria were labeled using a tetrazolium dye and sodium succinate before being layered onto a linear sucrose gradient. Enzyme assays, including NADH-dichlorophenolindo-phenol oxidoreductase, malate synthase, and isocitrate lyase, were conducted to measure enzyme activities. The study compared the effects of different cell disruption methods on the sedimentability of catalase and malate dehydrogenase. The specific activities and recovery of enzymes, such as urate oxidase, citrate synthase, glycollate oxidase, protein, D-Amino acid oxidase, adenylate kinase, acid phosphatase, alkaline phosphatase, and p-nitrophenylphosphate, were determined. The results and discussion, along with the sedimentability of catalase and malate dehydrogenase activities, are presented in Table 1 and Table 2. The process of cell disruption and the sedimentability of enzymes are illustrated in Figure 1.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Homogenize cells with a Potter Elvehjem homogenizer.
2. Add 0.035% Triton X-100 per 0.3 g of cells for 5 min before dilution.
3. Centrifuge at 1700 x g for 60 s to remove unbroken cells and cell fragments, obtaining the supernatant as the homogenate.
4. Determine the specific activity and sedimentability of enzymes in the homogenate.
5. Shorten the time of exposure to detergent to minimize organelle damage.
6. Use "sub-optimal" concentrations of Triton X-100 for longer periods to avoid reducing enzyme sedimentability.
7. Achieve the highest sedimentability by treating with 0.025% Triton X-100 for 10 s, but note that around one third of the cells may not be ruptured.
8. Use a compromise between yield and intactness of organelles.
9. Generally use 0.035% Triton X-100 and dilute after 10 s to rupture more than 85% of cells.
10. Verify that the supernatant following centrifugation at 1700 x g (60 s) is not contaminated by whole cells.
11. Perform differential centrifugation of homogenates to obtain fractions with specific activities and sedimentabilities of enzymes.

**Conclusion:**
The article describes the process of mitochondrial isolation using a Potter Elvehjem homogenizer and Triton X-100 detergent. The protocol includes homogenization, centrifugation to remove unbroken cells, determination of enzyme activity and sedimentability, and differential centrifugation to obtain fractions. However, the article does not provide complete details for each step, such as the speed and duration of homogenization or the specific centrifugation speeds and times for differential centrifugation.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Treatment Homogenate: The homogenate was subjected to centrifugation at 3000 x g, 20,000 x g, and 32,000 x g.
2. Supernatant Recovery: The supernatant was collected after each centrifugation step.
3. Catalase: Catalase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
4. Malate dehydrogenase: Malate dehydrogenase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
5. Urate oxidase: Urate oxidase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
6. Acid phosphatase: Acid phosphatase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
7. NADH-dichlorophenolindophenol oxidoreductase: NADH-dichlorophenolindophenol oxidoreductase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
8. Alkali phosphatase: Alkali phosphatase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
9. N-Acetylglucosaminidase: N-Acetylglucosaminidase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
10. Adenylate kinase: Adenylate kinase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
11. Citrate synthetase: Citrate synthetase activity was measured in the homogenate, 3000 x g sediment, 20,000 x g sediment, and 32,000 x g sediment.
12. Differential centrifugation: Homogenates treated with Triton X-100 were subjected to differential centrifugation at different speeds (3000 x g, 20,000 x g, and 32,000 x g). The highest activities of mitochondrial enzymes (malate dehydrogenase and citrate synthetase) were found in the 3000 x g sediment, while catalase activity was evenly spread between the 3000 x g and 20,000 x g sediments.
13. Distribution in density gradients: Malate dehydrogenase and catalase were loaded onto gradients after homogenization with a Teflon homogenizer. Peroxisomes remained intact during resuspension, while the majority of malate dehydrogenase was released from mitochondria either during resuspension or at the top of the gradient.
14. Recovery in density gradients: More than 90% of enzyme activity loaded was recovered in all gradients.
15. Treatment with Triton X-100: Gradients were loaded with fractions obtained after breaking cells with Triton X-100.

**Conclusion:** The article provides detailed steps for mitochondrial isolation, including centrifugation at different speeds, measurement of enzyme activities in different fractions, and the use of density gradients and Triton X-100 treatment. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, as it does not specify the homogenization method or the duration of centrifugation steps.

Enzyme activities and sedimentability were measured using different treatments and centrifugation speeds. Catalase, malate dehydrogenase, urate oxidase, acid phosphatase, NADH-dichloroindophenol, alkali phosphatase, N-acetylglucosaminidase, adenylate kinase, and citrate synthetase were examined. The distribution of enzyme activities in sucrose gradient fractions revealed the localization of peroxisomes and enzymes. Membrane-associated alkaline phosphatase and vacuole-associated N-acetylglucosaminidase and acid phosphatase were identified. Peroxisomes remained intact during resuspension, while mitochondria were less stable. Triton X-100 was used for cell lysis and enzyme extraction. Sucrose gradients were used for separation and sedimentation. Figure 2 illustrates the distribution of malate dehydrogenase and catalase in sucrose gradients.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:

1. Two peaks each of catalase and adenylate kinase activity are seen in gradients representing peroxisomes and mitochondria (lower peaks) and enzyme release at the 30% (w/v) sucrose "step" (upper peaks).
2. Very little enzyme activity was released during resuspension of sediments.
3. The mitochondria released no malate dehydrogenase during resuspension and also crossed the 30% (w/v) sucrose "step" without losing malate dehydrogenase activity.
4. Adenylate kinase is soluble between the outer and inner membranes of the mitochondria, while malate dehydrogenase is soluble in the internal matrix of the mitochondria.
5. The adenylate kinase, sequestered within a single membrane like catalase, is more readily released than the malate dehydrogenase.
6. Linear gradients from 15-50% (w/v) sucrose containing 2% (w/v) Ficoll are used, reducing the release of catalase at the top of the gradients.
7. The gradients show a separation of membranes containing acid and alkaline phosphatase, with alkaline phosphatase as a marker for the plasma membrane and acid phosphatase for the vacuole membranes.
8. "Heavy-labelling" of mitochondria is achieved using a tetrazolium dye and succinate. The mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase reduces the dye, and the product (formazan) is deposited on the mitochondrial membranes.
9. Gradients loaded with an untreated fraction show overlap of catalase and malate dehydrogenase activities, suggesting that over 60% of catalase activity could be mitochondria-associated.
10. After treatment with the dye and succinate, the lowest fractions of the gradient contain red-labeled membranes carrying the majority of malate dehydrogenase and NADH-dichlorophenolindophenol oxidoreductase activities.
11. Some catalase is found in the mitochondrial fractions, likely due to peroxisomes being "imprisoned" within the mitochondrial aggregates.
12. The sedimentability of catalase is largely unaffected after treatment, although the peak and shoulder effect seen in controls disappears, presumably due to peroxisomes in mitochondrial aggregates.
13. The treatment leads to a doubling of peroxisome breakage and release of catalase when the organelles enter the gradients. The mitochondria remain intact, retaining the majority of their malate dehydrogenase activity.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides several steps for mitochondrial isolation, including the use of gradients, enzyme release at the sucrose "step," and "heavy-labelling" of mitochondria. However, the protocol is incomplete, as it does not provide a detailed description of the specific isolation procedures and reagents used. Further information is needed to fully replicate the protocol.

summary: Three peaks of catalase and adenylate kinase activity are observed in gradients representing peroxisomes and mitochondria. Enzyme release occurs at the 30% sucrose "step." Little enzyme activity is released during resuspension of sediments. The location of mitochondrial enzymes is reflected in the results. Adenylate kinase is soluble between the outer and inner membranes, while malate dehydrogenase is soluble in the internal matrix of the mitochondria. Adenylate kinase is more readily released than malate dehydrogenase. The use of linear gradients with Ficoll reduces the release of catalase at the top of the gradients. Acid and alkaline phosphatase show separation in the gradients, with alkaline phosphatase as a marker for the plasma membrane and acid phosphatase for the vacuole membranes. "Heavy-labelling" of mitochondria using a tetrazolium dye and succinate is developed. The dye is reduced by mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase, depositing formazan on the mitochondrial membranes. Over 60% of catalase activity is associated with mitochondria. Treatment with the dye and succinate leads to the labeling of mitochondrial membranes. Peroxisomes are "imprisoned" within mitochondrial aggregates. Treatment increases peroxisome breakage and release of catalase. Mitochondria remain intact, retaining the majority of malate dehydrogenase activity.

The use of low concentrations of Triton X-100 
provides a good method for isolating intact organelles 
in high yield from Dictyostelium discoideum and other 
cellular slime moulds In comparison, cell homogeni- 
zation by physical means yields much poorer and more

sample then diluted to sub-optimal concentrations of 
detergent to protect organelles during centrifugation 
Sussman and Rayner [19] have also used Triton X-100

No catalase activity is associated with mitochondria and no malate dehydrogenase with peroxisomes. Urate oxidase is the only oxidase associated with catalase activity in gradients. Peroxisomes in Dictyostelium discoideum contain high activities of urate oxidase and no other peroxisomal enzymes. The cellular slime moulds could be useful for studying peroxisome synthesis and their role in metabolism.
